plan_summary: Baseline logistic regression model for Titanic survival binary classification with stratified 80/20 split
reviewer_reasoning: Iteration 1 establishes a solid baseline (AUC 0.835) with no risk flags, low overfitting, and no leakage. However, only one model family has been tried, 9 iterations remain, and tree-based models typically yield meaningful improvement on this dataset. Clear headroom exists.
router_reasoning: Natural progression from linear baseline to tree-based models. Logistic regression has limited capacity to capture non-linear interactions (e.g., Pclass x Fare, Age x Sex). Pivoting to gradient-boosted trees or random forest is the highest-value next step.

plan_summary: GradientBoosting (200 estimators, max_depth=4) with engineered features (Title, FamilySize) for Titanic binary classification
reviewer_reasoning: Primary metric (AUC-ROC) regressed from 0.835 to 0.822 (-0.013). While secondary metrics (accuracy +0.017, F1 +0.024, recall +0.029) improved slightly, the model exhibits severe overfitting: train AUC 0.993 vs validation 0.822, a 17.2% gap with a diverging learning curve. The GBM is memorising the training set rather than learning generalisable patterns. The overfitting severity (high) and metric regression make this iteration strictly worse than the baseline on the primary metric. Two model families tried (LogisticRegression, GradientBoosting); 8 iterations remain.
router_reasoning: Iteration 2 degraded the primary metric vs iteration 1 (best). The regression is caused by severe overfitting in the GBM, not by a code bug — the execution succeeded cleanly. Rolling back to iteration 1 as the base. The next plan should retry tree-based models with much stronger regularisation (lower max_depth, fewer estimators, higher min_samples_leaf, lower learning rate) or try RandomForest which is less prone to overfitting. The engineered features (Title, FamilySize) appear useful and should be retained.

risk_flags_summary.evidence: Train/val AUC-ROC gap of 0.171 (17.2%); learning curve diverging; train AUC 0.993 vs val 0.822

plan_summary: Regularised RandomForestClassifier (200 estimators, max_depth=5) with title-based and family-size features for Titanic binary classification
reviewer_reasoning: Iteration 3 is the new best model (AUC 0.8445, +0.0096 vs baseline, +0.0227 vs previous). Regularisation successfully reduced overfitting from 17.2% (high) to 6.5% (medium). No leakage detected. All secondary metrics improved except precision (-0.0006, negligible). Three model families tried (LR, GBM, RF) across 3 iterations with 7 remaining. The RF approach shows clear promise with room for hyperparameter refinement. Medium overfitting severity warrants continued monitoring but does not invalidate the result.
router_reasoning: Iteration 3 improved the primary metric and is the new best. The RandomForest regularisation strategy is working — overfitting dropped from high to medium, and AUC improved meaningfully. There is untapped potential in the current approach: tuning n_estimators, max_features, class_weight, or adding cross-validation. No plateau detected. Continue refining the RF approach before considering a pivot to XGBoost or stacking.

risk_flags_summary.evidence: Train/val AUC-ROC gap of 0.0582 (6.5%); learning curve trend diverging; train AUC 0.903 vs val 0.845. Improved from 17.2% in iteration 2.

plan_summary: Refined RandomForest with stronger regularisation (max_depth=4, min_samples_leaf=15, min_samples_split=30, 150 estimators) and Cabin deck-letter feature extraction for Titanic binary classification
reviewer_reasoning: Iteration 4 degraded the primary metric (AUC 0.841 vs 0.845, delta -0.004) and all secondary metrics worsened: accuracy -0.045, F1 -0.042, precision -0.077, recall unchanged. The stronger regularisation (max_depth 5->4, min_samples_leaf 10->15) reduced overfitting gap from 6.5% to 5.5% (still medium severity, learning curve still diverging), but at the cost of underfitting the validation set. The Cabin deck feature extraction added 6 new features but provided negligible signal (Deck_C through Deck_Rare all rank 15-21 with importance <0.002). Two consecutive RF iterations have now failed to surpass iteration 3. The 95% CI [0.766, 0.901] is wide (width 0.135), meaning the -0.004 regression is within noise, but the consistent decline across all secondary metrics suggests genuine degradation rather than random variation.
router_reasoning: Two RF iterations (3 and 4) have been explored, with iteration 4 degrading vs 3. The RF regularisation path is yielding diminishing returns. Three model families tried so far (LR, GBM, RF). The natural next step is gradient-boosted trees with proper regularisation (XGBoost or LightGBM with early stopping, low learning rate, and strong L1/L2 penalties) which can often outperform RF on structured data. The engineered features (Title, FamilySize, HasCabin) should be retained as they proved useful; the deck-letter features can be dropped. Alternatively, a blending/stacking ensemble of the best LR and RF models could capture complementary signals.

risk_flags_summary.evidence: Train/val AUC-ROC gap of 0.0488 (5.5%); learning curve trend diverging; train AUC 0.889 vs val 0.841. Slightly improved from 6.5% in iteration 3 but still medium severity.

plan_summary: GradientBoostingClassifier with conservative regularisation (lr=0.05, max_depth=3, min_samples_leaf=20, subsample=0.8, early stopping) and lean feature set (Title, FamilySize, HasCabin; dropped Cabin deck-letter features) — hypothesis: surpass iteration 3 AUC 0.8445 with tighter overfitting control.
reviewer_reasoning: Iteration 5 GBM (AUC 0.8387) did not beat iteration 3 RandomForest (best: 0.8445, delta -0.0058). Two consecutive iterations have now failed to improve on the best. Medium overfitting remains (8.62% train-val gap). Secondary metrics improved (accuracy +3.4%, F1 +1.7%, precision +8.6%) but primary metric regressed. The sklearn classifier family (LR, GBM, RF) is exhausted at this tuning depth. One clear unexplored avenue remains: XGBoost/LightGBM or stacking ensemble.
router_reasoning: Iterations 4 and 5 both failed to beat the iteration 3 best (AUC 0.8445). Sklearn-native gradient boosting is now exhausted. Pivot to XGBoost or LightGBM (more expressive gradient boosting with native regularisation and histogram-based splits) or a stacking ensemble anchored on iteration 3. Base the next plan on iteration 3 feature engineering as it produced the best result.

risk_flags_summary.evidence: Train/val AUC gap of 8.62% (train=0.9177, val=0.8387). Learning curve trend is plateau — further sklearn GBM tuning unlikely to close this gap.

plan_summary: StackingClassifier (RF + LR meta) with log-transformed Fare, HasCabin, Title, FamilySize features for Titanic binary classification
reviewer_reasoning: Iteration 6 is the new best model (AUC 0.8560, +0.0173 vs previous, +0.0115 vs prior best iter-3). Stacking broke through the 2-iteration plateau. Overfitting is low (4.2% train/val gap, down from 8.6% in iter-5). No leakage detected. All risk flags clear. However, the stacking approach has been introduced in this iteration only and has clear untapped potential: better base learners (XGBoost, LightGBM), meta-learner tuning, and cross-val stacking have not been explored. Continuing is warranted.
router_reasoning: Metric is improving and at a new best. Stacking is a newly introduced technique class with significant unexplored headroom: current base learners are RF + LR only, meta-learner is default LogisticRegression, no gradient-boosted base estimators tried. Adding XGBoost or LightGBM as base estimators and tuning the meta-learner is the highest-value next step.
